perturbation.original_text: Payments due shall be made within 45 days of the close of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments due shall be made within 90 days of the close of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timeframe to 90 days could potentially cause issues with prompt payment laws and industry standards which often require more expedited payment schedules. Delaying payment obligations beyond reasonable industry standards may be deemed unfair or violate good faith and fair dealing principles. Additionally, for small businesses, such delayed payments could impact cash flow, potentially in violation of state laws related to fair business practices.
perturbation.law_citation: MD. Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 15-104
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act typically requires payments to be made within 30 days unless a specific exception applies. Extending the payment timeframe to 90 days violates the spirit and possibly the letter of prompt payment laws designed to ensure timely compensation for goods and services. While this agreement is not directly with the State of Maryland, extending payment terms significantly beyond standard business practice (which the Prompt Payment Act reflects) may be viewed unfavorably or create legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates payments within 45 days, whereas the changed text extends this period to 90 days. According to the Law Explanation, this extended timeframe could violate the spirit, and possibly the letter, of prompt payment laws. The Perturbation Explanation further supports this by noting potential issues with prompt payment laws and industry standards, as well as possible violations of state laws related to fair business practices. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition is met because the changed text sets a deadline that likely violates state or federal law.

perturbation.original_text: No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than one (1) year after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than two (2) years from the date of the last payment.
perturbation.changed_text: No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than six (6) months after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than one (1) year from the date of the last payment.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to six months is problematic because it may violate Maryland's standard statute of limitations for contract disputes, which is typically longer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Statute of Limitations for Contract Actions
perturbation.law_citation: MD. Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 5-101
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law sets a three-year statute of limitations for contract actions. Reducing this to six months is generally unenforceable as it unreasonably restricts the time for parties to pursue legal remedies.

A civil action at law shall be filed within three years from the date it accrues unless another provision of the Code provides a different period of time within which an action shall be commenced. Previous Next Validation Please fix the following: OK Click to return on the top page
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for contract actions to six months, while Maryland law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, sets a three-year statute of limitations. This reduction directly contradicts the state law, making the contractual term unenforceable as it significantly restricts the legally provided time for pursuing legal remedies.

perturbation.original_text: Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at the rate of twelve percent (12.0%) per annum compounded monthly from the due date until such payment is received.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at the rate of twenty-five percent (25.0%) per annum compounded monthly from the due date until such payment is received.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the interest rate to 25% per annum may violate usury laws in Maryland, which set limits on the maximum permissible interest rates for loans and other financial transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland's usury laws restrict the amount of interest that can be charged on loans and other financial transactions. Charging 25% interest, depending on the specifics of the transaction and the parties involved, is very likely to exceed the legal limit and be deemed usurious, making the interest provision unenforceable.

(a) (1) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) of this section, a lender may charge interest at an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 8 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if there is a written agreement signed by the borrower which sets forth the stated rate of interest charged by the lender. (2) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is a certificate of deposit held by the borrower, the lender may charge interest at a rate not to exceed 2 percent in excess of the rate of interest payable on the certificate of deposit. (3) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is other than a savings account or if such loan is unsecured, the lender may charge a rate of interest not in excess of 18 percent. However, on a loan made on or after July 1, 1982, a lender may charge an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 24 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance, provided that: (i) If the loan is a renewal or refinancing of a loan made prior to July 1, 1982, the lender complies with § 12–116 of this subtitle; (ii) If the loan includes a provision for a rate of interest which may be adjusted by the lender during the term of the loan, the lender complies with § 12–118 of this subtitle; (iii) Upon the borrower’s default, if the loan is secured by personal property, the lender complies with § 12–115 of this subtitle concerning repossession and redemption of the goods securing the loan; (iv) If the loan is for the purchase of consumer goods, the loan contract complies with § 12–117 of this subtitle; and (v) The loan does not include a balloon payment, unless payment in full is due on demand or in one year or less. (b) (1) A lender may charge interest at any effective rate of simple interest on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if: (i) There is a written agreement signed by the borrow
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate from 12% to 25% per annum. The Law Explanation suggests that charging 25% interest could violate Maryland's usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that a lender may charge interest at an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 8 percent per year, or in some instances up to 24 percent per year, depending on the loan type and conditions. The change to 25% therefore creates a potential violation of usury laws, constituting a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports that this increase can violate usury laws.
